channel.title: Prerna Tyagi
channel.description: Applied AI/ML engineering — implementations, experiments, builds

item.title: 01 · Embeddings & vector search
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Part 1 of Building Blocks of Modern AI — in progress. This post is being written in public, section by section. Companion code: github.com/tyagiprerna0808/ml-applied-depth. Last update: 2026-05-28.

The question

Given a corpus of millions of documents and a query, return the top-k nearest neighbors in under 10 ms with recall@10 > 0.95, on a laptop. How does that actually work?

Roadmap

  1. ✅ What an embedding is (first taste)
  2. ⏳ Similarity metrics — cosine vs dot vs L2
  3. ✅ Brute-force baseline on a real corpus
  4. ⏳ The curse of dimensionality
  5. ⏳ Coming next — post 02 · ANN from scratch (HNSW, IVF, PQ)

✅ A first taste

Code: blog/01-embeddings/hello_embeddings.py

Four sentences — two about gardening, two about finance. The model (sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-d) never sees the topic labels.

  0.622   'I love plants'              vs  'Gardening is fun'             ← gardening
  0.149   'The stock market crashed'   vs  'Bonds and treasury yields'    ← finance
  0.021   'I love plants'              vs  'The stock market crashed'
  0.016   'I love plants'              vs  'Bonds and treasury yields'
  0.015   'Gardening is fun'           vs  'The stock market crashed'
  0.005   'Gardening is fun'           vs  'Bonds and treasury yields'

Cross-pairs score 30× to 120× lower than the same-topic pairs.

The gardening pair (0.62) outscores the finance pair (0.15) — both topical neighbors, but “I love plants” and “Gardening is fun” are closer in sentiment-and-meaning than “stock market crashed” (a panic event) and “bonds and treasury yields” (fixed-income instruments). Embeddings reflect meaning, not just topic labels.

✅ Brute-force search on 5,000 news passages

Code: blog/01-embeddings/search_corpus.py

Corpus: 5,000 passages from fancyzhx/ag_news. Embeddings cached to disk after the first run. Top-k via one matmul per query against the L2-normalized corpus matrix.

Sample query: “tennis player wins grand slam”. Top result:

Roddick escapes, but not Federer. ATHENS, Greece. Andy Roddick #39;s biggest obstacle to winning the gold medal was Roger Federer…

The phrase “grand slam” does not appear in the passage. The embedding placed it as the closest match because Roddick + Federer + Olympics + gold medal sits at the right point in tennis-meaning-space.

Another query: “earnings report from a tech company”. The third match was Limited Brands — a clothing retailer with earnings reports. The embedding weighted earnings report heavily and partially missed tech company; when a query bundles multiple concepts, the embedding averages them and one concept can dominate. This is exactly why production RAG adds reranking, query rewriting, and hybrid sparse+dense search.

Steady-state search latency at N=5,000: ~0.7 ms per query (single CPU core).

✅ Latency scaling — when brute force breaks

Code: blog/01-embeddings/latency_scaling.py

Measured: K=10, 50 queries per N, 384-d vectors, single CPU core (Intel i5-9300H), L2-normalized matmul.

N mean (ms) p95 (ms)
100 0.017 0.021
500 0.033 0.036
1,000 0.052 0.067
2,500 0.279 0.440
5,000 0.605 0.857

Past N ≈ 1,000 the curve is linear: ~121 ns per document. Linear extrapolation:

Latency budget Brute-force ceiling
10 ms ~83,000 docs
100 ms ~830,000 docs
1 s ~8.3 million docs

For reference: English Wikipedia is ≈ 6 million articles, MS MARCO is ≈ 8.8 million passages. Brute force can’t hit a 10 ms production budget on either; HNSW, IVF, and PQ exist to climb past this wall — covered in post 02.

⏳ Coming next

Post 02 — ANN data structures from scratch (HNSW, IVF, PQ). Same 5K corpus, target ~100× latency cut at ≥95% recall vs. the brute-force baseline above.

Working notes

Session-1 raw notes: notes/post-01-session-1.md. Tutor review of those notes — including the math errors caught: notes/post-01-session-1-review.md.

item.title: 02 · Vector search — ANN from scratch
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI. Companion code lands at blog/02-vector-search-ann/ when work starts.

Planned coverage

  • The wall — brute force breaks past ~80K docs at a 10 ms latency budget (extrapolated from post 01)
  • IVF (inverted file index) — k-means partitioning, probe count, recall/latency tradeoff, from scratch
  • PQ (product quantization) — codebooks, asymmetric distance computation, from scratch
  • HNSW (hierarchical navigable small world) — graph construction, greedy descent, the M and efSearch knobs, from scratch
  • Head-to-head — brute force vs IVF vs PQ vs HNSW vs FAISS reference, all measured on the same 5K corpus
  • When to use what — in-process vs managed (Pinecone / Qdrant / Weaviate) and where the cliff is

item.title: 03 · Tokenization
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI.

Planned coverage

  • Why tokenization matters — what changes if you get it wrong (multilingual, code, math)
  • Character-level vs word-level — the extremes and what each costs
  • BPE (byte-pair encoding) from scratch — the algorithm GPT / RoBERTa / Llama use
  • WordPiece — what BERT does differently and why
  • SentencePiece + Unigram — Google’s approach for multilingual
  • Comparing tokenizer outputs on the same multilingual text
  • Production considerations — vocab size, OOV handling, multilingual tradeoffs, byte-fallback

item.title: 04 · Attention from scratch
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI.

Planned coverage

  • The intuition — weighted average of values, weighted by similarity of queries to keys
  • Q, K, V — what each represents and why three projections, not one
  • Scaled dot-product attention — the formula derived; the √d_k scaling and what breaks without it
  • Multi-head attention — what splitting into heads actually buys you
  • Variants — MQA, GQA, MLA at a glance — why production LLMs use them
  • Positional information — sinusoidal and RoPE, in this post
  • Implementation from scratch in NumPy, then in PyTorch with shape-by-shape commentary

item.title: 05 · Loss, optimization, backpropagation
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI. This post is the literal mechanism behind the signature line — loss decreases with optimized iterations is the algorithm.

Planned coverage

  • The loss function family — MSE, cross-entropy, contrastive — when each is right
  • Gradient descent — vanilla, SGD, mini-batch
  • Backpropagation — chain rule, derived end-to-end on a 2-layer MLP
  • Adam, AdamW, momentum — what each fixes vs vanilla SGD
  • Learning rate schedules — warmup, cosine; the production defaults
  • Vanishing / exploding gradients — why they happen, what fixes them
  • From-scratch implementation in NumPy, then PyTorch

item.title: 06 · Fine-tuning fundamentals — LoRA
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. First application post in Building Blocks of Modern AI — builds directly on attention (04) and optimization (05).

Planned coverage

  • The fine-tuning question — when does it beat a well-engineered prompt
  • Full fine-tuning — what it costs (compute, memory, dataset, eval discipline)
  • LoRA — the low-rank decomposition math, why r=8 is a workable default
  • QLoRA — what 4-bit quantization buys you in memory
  • Practical workflow on a small open model (TinyLlama or similar) — end to end
  • Eval-driven iteration — picking when to stop training

item.title: 07 · Retrieval & RAG
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI. Builds on embeddings (01) and ANN (02).

Planned coverage

  • BM25 from scratch — the keyword baseline that still wins more than people admit
  • Dense retrieval — extending post 01’s embeddings to a real corpus
  • Hybrid scoring — reciprocal rank fusion, weighted combination
  • Cross-encoder reranking — when it helps, when it hurts
  • RAG end-to-end on a real corpus, measured
  • Failure modes — query rewriting, multi-hop, lost-in-the-middle, citation faithfulness
  • The gap between “RAG works on a demo” and “RAG works in production”

item.title: 08 · Evals for generative systems
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Part of Building Blocks of Modern AI.

Planned coverage

  • Why classical metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) fail for generative tasks
  • Golden sets — building, curating, versioning
  • LLM-as-judge — pairwise vs pointwise, calibration against humans
  • Rubric design — what to measure beyond “is it good”
  • A regression harness you can drop into CI
  • Cost vs signal — cheaper judges, bigger judges, when each makes sense

item.title: 09 · Agent loops & tool use
item.dc:creator: Prerna Tyagi
item.description:
← series

Planned. Final post of Building Blocks of Modern AI — pulls earlier blocks together into a working loop.

Planned coverage

  • The basic loop — observe, think, act, observe, repeat
  • ReAct from scratch — without LangChain
  • Tool calling — function-calling APIs and what the JSON schema actually gives you
  • Planning vs reacting — when each pattern is right
  • Error recovery — what happens when the tool fails (and how often it does)
  • Short-term context vs long-term memory
  • The gap between “works in a demo” and “works in production”
